critic_raw:
The data splitting strategy is the primary methodological failure. The design uses stratified random sampling across all 60 months of data, explicitly holding out 'a random 15% of observations as the test set.' In a 60-month longitudinal EHR dataset, a patient may have multiple admissions across the study period. Randomly splitting at the encounter level—rather than the patient level—virtually guarantees that multiple encounters from the same patient will appear in both the training and test sets. Because the features include prior admission counts, utilization history, and demographic variables, and because readmission risk is highly patient-specific (some patients are chronic high-risk readmitters), this patient-level leakage will systematically inflate performance estimates. The design explicitly mentions 'patient-level stratification' but then stratifies at the encounter level ('across all available months').

The temporal dimension is completely abandoned. A 60-month study window contains meaningful concept drift: hospital protocols change, patient mix evolves, coding practices shift, and readmission definitions under CMS evolve. Using a random split means training and test sets are contemporaneous, which does not represent the deployment scenario in which the model is applied prospectively to new discharges. A temporal split is the methodologically appropriate choice for this setting and would expose whether the model's learned representations degrade over time.

The multi-task design introduces a secondary task (LOS regression) ostensibly to regularize learned representations, but the design does not adequately control for whether any performance advantage derives from this auxiliary task or from the multi-modal architecture. The correct ablation would include: (1) single-task deep learning on identical features, (2) multi-task on structured features only, (3) full multi-task. Without these ablations, it is impossible to attribute gains to any specific architectural decision.

Finally, the use of a pre-trained clinical BERT model with fine-tuning raises the possibility of benchmark contamination. If clinical BERT was pre-trained on notes from the same hospital system (or similar EHR systems), the model's representations encode distributional knowledge that logistic regression does not have access to, making the comparison unfair regardless of preprocessing parity.
defender_raw:
This multi-task deep learning design for readmission prediction demonstrates careful attention to the most common failure modes in clinical ML. The cohort definition is precise: index admissions within rolling 60-day windows prevent double-counting of re-admissions as independent prediction episodes, and the threshold for note inclusion (≥50 tokens) excludes trivially sparse records that would inject noise into a text-based model without providing usable signal. The conservative imputation choices — comorbidity flags as absent, utilization as zero — err on the side of not inventing clinical information, which is the right default in a prediction-focused (not causal) study.

The multi-task architecture is justified on principled grounds: clinical notes encode information about both readmission risk and treatment response pathways, and jointly optimizing for both targets encourages the model to learn more generalizable patient representations rather than overfitting to the narrow readmission label distribution. The use of a transformer trained on structured features plus text embeddings versus a logistic regression baseline provides a fair test of the multi-modal and multi-task contributions simultaneously, with the task framing explicitly operationalizing 'efficiency' as higher AUROC at lower parameter count.

The evaluation design is clinically anchored. Reporting sensitivity at 90% specificity, AUPRC, and calibration error alongside AUROC gives a complete picture of clinical utility rather than a single optimistic metric. Department-level stratification in the controls directly addresses the concern that readmission rates vary systematically by unit and admitting team, ensuring that model gains are not artifacts of imbalanced department representation in splits. The explicit scope statement — that the design establishes comparative predictive accuracy, not causality or clinical efficacy — sets appropriate expectations and prevents over-interpretation.
all_issues_raised: Random encounter-level split with 60 months of multi-visit patient data almost certainly allows the same patient to appear in train and test, inflating estimates via patient-level leakage
Stratified random split across the full timeline abandons temporal order, failing to simulate the prospective deployment scenario and obscuring concept drift
Multi-task design confounds architecture with auxiliary task regularization — no ablation distinguishes LOS-sharing from multi-modal text benefits
Clinical BERT pre-trained on overlapping or similar EHR corpora creates an unfair informational advantage over logistic regression that preprocessing parity cannot correct

15% test set drawn from 60 months of data provides no guarantee of temporal breadth — the held-out set may cluster in certain months, failing to represent performance across the full operational window
all_issues_adjudicated: Random encounter-level split with 60 months of multi-visit patient data almost certainly allows the same patient to appear in train and test, inflating estimates via patient-level leakage
Stratified random split across the full timeline abandons temporal order, failing to simulate the prospective deployment scenario and obscuring concept drift
